spans.description:
SELECT chatbots.id 
FROM chatbots 
WHERE chatbots.organization_id = $1::VARCHAR AND chatbots.chatbot_id = $2::VARCHAR AND chatbots.is_active IS true AND ((EXISTS (SELECT 1 
FROM users, user_chatbots 
WHERE chatbots.id = user_chatbots.chatbot_id AND users.id = user_chatbots.user_id AND users.username = $3::VARCHAR)) OR (EXISTS (SELECT 1 
FROM user_domains, user_domain_chatbots 
WHERE chatbots.id = user_domain_chatbots.chatbot_id AND user_domains.id = user_domain_chatbots.user_domain_id AND (EXISTS (SELECT 1 
FROM users 
WHERE user_domains.id = users.user_domain_id AND users.username = $4::VARCHAR)))) OR (EXISTS (SELECT 1 
FROM teams, team_chatbots 
WHERE chatbots.id = team_chatbots.chatbot_id AND teams.id = team_chatbots.team_id AND (EXISTS (SELECT 1 
FROM users, user_teams 
WHERE teams.id = user_teams.team_id AND users.id = user_teams.user_id AND users.username = $5::VARCHAR))))) 
 LIMIT $6::INTEGER

data.gen_ai.output.messages:
[{"role": "assistant", "parts": [{"type": "text", "content": "Main point: This document is a complete requirements specification for building a basic web calculator. It defines what the app must do (functional), how it should behave and look (non‑functional), technical constraints, testing, risks, and acceptance criteria so developers, testers, and stakeholders can implement and validate the product.

Concise summary by section:

1. Overview
- Purpose: Build a simple, reliable calculator for desktop and mobile browsers that performs core arithmetic and is easy to use.
- Scope: Core functionality only (foundation for future enhancements).

2. Functional requirements
- Basic operations: Support addition, subtraction, multiplication, division with decimals and negatives; handle formatting and precision; guard against division by zero.
- UI elements: Primary display (current input/result), optional secondary display (current operation), numeric keypad (0–9), operation buttons (+, -, *, /), control buttons (C, =, ., optional AC).
- Layout/design: Grid-based, accessible, touch-friendly, consistent styling.
- Calculation behavior: Multi-digit and decimal input, single decimal point rule, prevent invalid formats, process one operator at a time but allow chaining using previous results, format large/small numbers (scientific notation if needed), round/trim decimals appropriately.

3. Non-functional requirements
- Usability/accessibility: Intuitive layout, minimum touch target (≥44px), high contrast, keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, screen-reader support.
- Performance: Instant calculations (<100 ms), app load <2s, immediate visual feedback, low memory/CPU footprint.
- Error handling: Detect and clearly report division-by-zero and invalid input sequences; recover gracefully.
- Compatibility: Support modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) and responsive behavior across desktop/tablet/mobile breakpoints.

4. Technical specifications
- Implementation: Pure HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript (no frameworks required); optional PWA and local storage for preferences.
- Code quality: Modular, documented, maintainable, robust error handling.
- Testing: Unit tests for calculation logic, integration tests for UI, cross-browser and mobile testing.

5. Security and privacy
- All calculations local in the browser; no personal data collection or external API calls.

6. Out of scope / Future enhancements
- Memory/scientific/statistical functions, history, themes, parentheses/multi-step expressions, graphing, etc. — reserved for later versions.

7. Acceptance criteria
- App is complete when arithmetic works correctly, UI is responsive and accessible, error handling and cross-browser behavior are verified, and performance/test criteria pass.

8. Development approach & timeline
- Iterative, test-driven recommended.
- Suggested phases: core logic (week 1–2), UI (week 2–3), testing/refinement (week 3–4).

9. QA standards
- Peer code review and high test coverage (target ≥90% for critical functions).

10. Deployment & maintenance
- Deployable on standard web hosting; include minified assets and a release checklist. Plan for updates to address browser changes and user feedback.

11. Risks & mitigations
- Browser compatibility, floating-point precision in JS (suggest using libraries or custom handling), performance on low-end devices, and accessibility/design risks. Mitigation: testing matrix, progressive enhancement, precision handling, profiling and user testing.

12. Internationalization
- Design to accommodate different decimal/grouping conventions and RTL cultures in future; use UTF-8 and validate input from different keyboards.

Quick recommendations (practical next steps)
- Implement core arithmetic functions with thorough unit tests first.
- Use a decimal/precision library (or integer-based approach) to avoid JS floating-point surprises.
- Build accessible markup (semantic elements, ARIA) and ensure 44px touch targets.
- Create automated tests for cross-browser and mobile responsiveness.
- Add a short user-acceptance test plan mapping to the Acceptance Criteria.

If you want, I can:
- Convert this into a one-page requirements checklist for developers/testers.
- Propose a minimal HTML/CSS/JS prototype outline that satisfies these requirements.
- Create example unit tests (e.g., Jest) for the arithmetic functions."}], "finish_reason": "stop"}]
